data.title: Corning: An Unexpected Picks And Shovels Option In AI
data.description:
Corning's (NYSE:GLW) Springboard plan, launched in 2024, has built authentic real value for its shareholders, and now that the strategy has delivered, I think success chances are quite legitimate, and shareholders will continue to benefit from these shares. The stock has crushed the S&P 500 (SPY), being up 63% compared to a good 27% return from the general index, thanks greatly to the AI revolution and the good management capitalizing on it.

The company has very good levels of innovation, a decent balance sheet, and a good management team. This, combined with a solid business model that is able to grow constantly and steadily, would bring good capital appreciation to shareholders.

Corning, Infrastructural Importance in Technology

GLW's business model is simple, it is a B2B company that manufactures critical products based on materials with unique characteristics. Its solutions are usually difficult to replicate by competitors and not easily scalable.

The company reports in five segments:

1. Optical Communications: Focused on optical fiber, carriers, and components that provide infrastructure for telecommunication networks. Optical fiber is still a critical product for meeting the increasing interconnectivity demand and is usually preferred over copper alternatives due to being more efficient and cost-effective. Because of this, its applications inside the AI plumbing ecosystem are vital, especially when increasing capacities inside data centers. The segment represents 32% of total revenues and grew 16% in 2024.

2. Display Technologies: This segment is based on selling glass substrates. A glass substrate is an extremely thin layer on which different technologies are set. Its main applications are in OLEDs and LCDs, and it is basically an irreplaceable component in this industry. This segment represents 26% of revenues but is extremely profitable, providing almost 45% of earnings in 2024.

3. Specialty Materials: This division is about many of Corning's most famous applications, like the Corning Gorilla Glass, which is the chemically strengthened glass used in many smartphones. Other applications include aerospace and defense optics, radiation shielding, and sunglasses. It represented 14% of Corning's revenues last year.

4. Environmental Technologies: Here, Corning manufactures filters and ceramic substrate products that help reduce emissions by being present in catalytic converters in ICE vehicles worldwide. This segment is small and represents only 11.4% of total revenues in 2024.

5. Life Sciences: The life sciences segment sales are based on laboratory consumables like handling vessels, liquid handling plastics, specialty surfaces, and general equipment. This segment is the smallest in the company, with only 6.7% of revenues last year. In my view, this is the most commoditized segment in Corning, and it is reflected in the razor-thin margins of less than 7%, representing just $63 million in earnings, and less than 3% of Corning's total earnings.

The Springboard plan, the materialization of the new AI increased demand

In 2024, Corning's management revealed a new strategy to capitalize on new opportunities surging from the AI boom. That strategy is called the Springboard plan and is centered mainly on offering new innovative products. This plan expects to increase revenues for GLW from $13 billion at the end of 2023 to $19 billion at the end of 2026 and is very likely to reach $21 billion by the end of 2028, while also expanding operating margins from around 18% to 20.5%, increasing profitability.

A noticeable part of this plan is reflected in increasing the optical communications revenues. Other segments also benefit from the Springboard plan but are far from being in the plan's highlights. For example, in the last Q1 2025 earnings results, the company saw 13% revenue growth and 42% core net income growth. In nominal terms, the respective increases were $421 million and $137 million, of which $425 million in revenues came from the Gen AI products in optical fiber, resulting in a $100 million increase in new net income. It is worth noticing that some business segments saw less revenue due to cyclical patterns, like automotive, down 10% year over year.

I consider this Springboard plan another form of operating leverage because, as mentioned by the company at this conference, much of the new incoming revenue is a product of innovation and leveraging the current installed capacity inside the company, instead of increasing the headcount, marketing expenses, or squeezing new market share. I like this approach, and it is quite valid in my view.

The company is laser-focused on increasing its competitive advantages via constant innovation, making it quite difficult for competitors to follow in its footsteps quickly. Because of GLW being, after all, an industrial company, manufacturing physical stuff, instead of directly offering services like software or financial companies, when the company innovates, an adequate response from competition might be delayed enough that Corning ends up taking a leader, undisputable market share position.

Some of the current Springboard trends are, for example, in the inevitable increased demand for optical fiber inside Gen AI data centers. As the company explains, in the escalation of the creation of new GPU nodes, many of the copper connections might eventually be replaced by optical fiber, which is cheaper and more efficient as the systems become bigger and more complex. Here, Corning not only sells more optical fiber but also innovates by increasing the density of its fiber up to 4 times.

Other important trends inside the Springboard plan are the increased screen size by an average of one inch a year in TVs, according to the most recent earnings call. The company is also increasing the prices of its glass substrates, which will allow increased margins from this already very profitable segment. This also shows some pricing power, a good sign of a strong market position from the company.

There are other initiatives in the Springboard plan, but they are, in my opinion, way less tangible. For example, the company is trying to dabble in the solar energy space, manufacturing in the US, and leveraging its expertise in materials science.

The Springboard plan has had a nice success, and now the company expects, with reasonable confidence, not only $6 billion but $8 billion of increased revenue by the end of 2026, as 80% of its 5-year production fiber capacity is already sold.

Some GLW's Financial Qualities

I consider Corning a solid company overall. Its free cash flow margin corresponds to around 10% of revenues, which is impressive for a company that should not be enjoying this kind of margin according to the products it sells. This talks very well about the quality and competitive advantages that the company has in the industry.

From a balance sheet perspective, a first glance suggests that GLW has tons of debt, around $7 billion long-term, which is quite high compared to the current cash flows of just $1.4 billion. Fortunately, Corning's debt structure is quite particular and not really that alarming, as much of its debt is offered in the form of debentures with maturities dosed from 2029 until 2079, which represents not really a threat for the company. There are other debentures denominated in yen, which are of just $463 million before 2030 ends, so, while the company is indeed in tons of debt, this time, because of the long maturities, is quite manageable.

The company is also not in financial distress currently, with current assets of more than $7.6 billion and current liabilities of just $4.5 billion. The company has made some buybacks, but since 2018, those have only covered the share dilution on average. The company does have a good track of dividend payments, being around 75% of the cash flows, which is currently close to 2.5%

Valuation

I think Corning is growing faster than the market is valuing it currently. At a forward PE of 20, and the company announcing continued excess growth until 2028, this number might be higher in my view.

In the following model, I assume an optimistic, but I think still realistic, view. Considering the revenue projections and margin expansion that the company has presented, and also taking into account the strong fiber demand, I estimate that by 2028, the company should have earnings of around $2.4 billion. I also think that this margin expansion and growth should lead to a higher PE of 24 in an optimistic market.

I am not counting dividends or buybacks in this capital appreciation framework. As a result, an investor might get a 9.6% CAGR without additional shareholder returns from the company, thanks to the Springboard plan.

Risks

Corning is a very solid business across the board, and thanks to its capital intensive and expertise required, it might be very difficult to disrupt. The company has been alive for more than 170 years, and currently, it is still quite resilient.

Some risks in Corning are very well mitigated, like, for example, having a low impact from tariffs and general geopolitical uncertainty. The company has, nonetheless, macroeconomic exposure and lower demand for TVs, smartphones, and catalytic converters because of higher EV demand, or even smaller-than-expected demand for AI optical fiber products, which might significantly affect margins and therefore, the investment thesis.

Takeaway

Corning is a solid growth company at this point. GLW has an attractive share price, stable income, strong demand for its products, strong margins, and great management.

Although the company has some debt and still cannot grow massively like a software company, Corning is an almost irreplaceable participant in the current technological environment, and at this moment, I consider it a solid buy for long-term investors with diversified portfolios.

data.title: S&P500: Rising Yields, Stalling Trade Talks, Escalating Geopolitics - Sell
data.description:
It's back to Sell

Last week I upgraded S&P500 (SP500) to 'Hold' from 'Sell,' but only briefly as I re-recommended a Sell just a few days ago. This is a highly volatile environment, which requires a very quick reaction - and the situation has fundamentally deteriorated since last week.

To refresh, last week, I noted that:

  • The recession had been delayed due to the US-China trade war de-escalation.
  • Inflation remained contained, based on the recent CPI report.
  • The AI trade has reemerged after Trump's dealmaking during his visit to the Middle East.

Thus, with the assumption that the described environment holds, I even suggested that I remain open to buy the dip. We have the dip now, but the environment has changed, and this is not a buyable dip, it's actually the opportunity to sell, as the current selloff is likely to deepen.

Yields are rising - that's not good

What's new this week is that Trump's big beautiful bill has passed the House, and it looks like it will add to the deficit more than expected. That could be good news, since it could boost growth more than expected.

However, the bond market doesn't like it, and as a result the long-term Treasury Bond yields (TLT) are rising - and this is negative for the stock market. In fact, this is a highly worrying sign given that Moody's downgraded US debt this Friday specifically due to the expected rising deficit with Trump's bill.

So, the major expected positive from Trump's pro-growth plan is now actually turning negative - so what's the positive for that market then? Nothing.

But it's not only the US yields that are rising, the Japanese yields are rising as well, which increases the risk of a major liquidity-shock driven selloff - and that was the reason for my downgrade to sell few days ago.

Stalling trade talks

Last week the market was full of optimism that the US-China trade war de-escalation would delay or even cancel the expected inflationary recession. The idea was that Trump tariffs would settle at the 10% universal tariff, 30% tariff on China, and some additional sector-specific tariffs - and this should be mild enough to prevent stagflation.

However, China is already accusing the US of breaching the "consensus" reached in Geneva, and it has to do with the ban on Huawei chips. As CNN reports:

China’s Commerce Ministry fired its latest broadside, accusing the US of “abusing export controls to suppress and contain China” and engaging in what it called “typical acts of unilateral bullying and protectionism.”

The fact is that the US has to try to contain China, that's the main motive behind the tariffs - and the main objective from China. Thus, the US-China trade deal is very unlikely, and the reciprocal tariffs are likely to be re-imposed after the 90-day truce.

In addition, it seems like the trade negotiations with other countries are stalling as well. Treasury Secretary Bessent warned that the reciprocal tariffs will be reimposed, and urged the trading partners to accelerate the negotiations, and warned that some countries will get the letter notifying of the increase in tariffs "within weeks".

The negotiations with Japan, South Korea are also stalling, in the third or fourth round of negotiations. This means that the US demands must be unrealistic.

The collapse of trade war negotiations and the return to reciprocal tariffs on some level brings back the stagflationary scenario. The AI theme could be crushed by a recession, as this will have to affect the capex.

Escalation geopolitics

Last week was very positive with respect to geopolitics. First, President Trump announced that Iran has "mostly agreed" to the nuclear deal. This would be excellent news because it would remove the risk of oil prices spiking, as well as promote peace in the region. Thus, I even downgraded Oil (CL1:COM) from Buy to Hold.

However, I was skeptical that the deal could be reached, and specifically noted that any deal that allows Iran to continue to enrich uranium could prompt Israel to target Iran's nuclear infrastructure, all by itself. That's in-fact what it is seems like it's happening, given the reports that Israel is preparing an attack on Iran's nuclear facilities. Thus, the Iran nuclear situation has significantly escalated this week.

Second, last week Trump announced a phone call with Putin, and the hope was that this could result in a cease-fire in Ukraine. However, nothing came out of the long phone call on Monday. In fact, Trump admitted that Putin does not want peace because he's "winning". So, the situation in Ukraine significantly deteriorated this week, as now it's obvious that the war continues.

Implications

The fundamentals have significantly deteriorated this week compared to the widespread optimism last week.

  • The US Treasuries got downgraded, and the yields are rising due to the expected increase in debt and deficit due to Trump's big beautiful bill. This is important because it negates the only positive from the Trump policy - the pro-growth agenda. The yields are rising globally - and this all makes it very risky to hold stocks, as the liquidity could dry out and cause a sharp selloff.
  • The trade talks are stalling, and the US-China trade deal seems to be very elusive. Thus, it looks like the reciprocal tariffs are likely to be re-imposed, and this brings back the stagflationary scenario - the worst scenario for the stock market.
  • The geopolitical situation is escalating with the Iran nuclear situation, as well as the failed attempt to force a ceasefire in Ukraine. This means that a possible oil price spike is back on - and that's the last thing we need in a stagflationary environment.

Thus, the bear market rally in the S&P500 (SPX) was likely stalled last week with the peak optimism, and the downtrend is likely to continue. Note, this is the situation this week.

If the current situation holds, I see the S&P500 revisiting the 200dma at 5770, and once the 200dma is broken, I expect the sharp selloff to the previous low around 4800-5000.

However, the situation could change again next week. We could also have a major trade deal announced, although it seems unlikely. We also have Nvidia (NVDA) earnings next week, and this event could provide support at the 200dma, or cause the 200dma breakdown. I think that the breakdown is more likely - but again I am open to buying-the-dip at 200dma if appropriate. Right now, I am Short SPX.

data.title: Axon Enterprise: Enormous Capabilities, But At A Price
data.description:
Introduction

As the public safety landscape has evolved over the last few years, Axon Enterprise (AXON) has emerged as a major player, bringing the well-known TASER brand alongside impressive technology in the body-cam space, and linking to cloud software capabilities in a slick ecosystem which looks capable of generating high-margins for the business.

Shares have impressed the market of late, soaring well above 100% in the last year, driven by innovative products in the AI and UAV space, as well as plans to expand globally. However, with shares now trading at fairly elevated levels, questions remain on whether the valuation still makes sense. Few would debate this being one of the ones to watch in the public security space, but I'm not sure new investors have a huge amount of upside potential for now. The operation is clearly doing all the right things, but the market may need to cool off for a while, resulting in my Hold rating.

Company Overview

The company originated as TASER International over 30 years ago, rebranding to Axon Enterprise in 2017 as it looked to expand the product offering beyond this iconic product.

As with many firms in the market, data appears to be one of the major currencies the company deals in, arguably even more than in the products it sells to long-term public partners. The Software division now commands a huge presence in the body camera space, bringing transparency and validation tools to public safety teams, as well as providing a platform for tracking evidence, and managing vehicle systems.

As an investor, I'd be pleased to see a rather diverse and widespread offering of hardware and software, clearly making a huge impact on the way law enforcement and other agencies go about their jobs. With the number of lives saved, and the amount of data being tracked by management, it's clear that the products work, and there is enormous demand for such systems.

Given recent and historical events in the emergency services, it's no surprise to see there being a huge demand for tools which can provide safety to teams, and to the public, as well as tracking the objective reality of events as they happen. With these tools, such as body-cams in place, enormous revenue opportunities present themselves in the form of long-term subscriptions. Building this platform ensures agencies are likely to build lasting relationships, and use the same vertical integration for other tactical systems, such as evidence documentation, fleet management, and team tracking.

Strategic Plan

I'm impressed by what I see at the domestic level, but management clearly has plans to push this globally. Axon President Josh noted recently that international demand was at a peak. Building a highly capable and experienced management team has clearly been crucial to this story, especially in areas where adoption rates have historically been poor, such as Europe, where there is still some hesitancy for cloud-based data for public safety groups.

Of course, this hesitancy has somewhat changed in some arenas, most notably in the necessity for drone systems in Ukraine. By acquiring Dedrone, Axon has clearly moved into this sector, deploying technology on the battlefield in Eastern Ukraine.

Management clearly see this as a potentially massive market, where defence spending is likely to continue surging amid heightened geopolitical conflict, pushing areas which were nowhere near the scope of the company well into play.

Strong Demand

It feels like a pretty uncertain time in the wider economy, but from the long-term trend, public safety spending feels fairly immune to general volatility. Despite some political pushback at times, investment in public safety seems to be a fairly bipartisan topic, with stimulus and funding seemingly available regardless of the party in power over the last few decades.

Looking beyond government and public sector work, Axon has been able to penetrate the private markets fairly well, with retail outlets looking to push back on increasing amounts of theft and abuse of staff. As many will know, shoplifting has soared across the retail sector of late, again bringing opportunities for Axon's subscription-based model to expand. These systems are cheap and easy to adopt for companies, and also deliver a high return on investment through the reduction of these shoplifting incidents.

From my perspective, the company's Draft One product is the next big thing, with over $100 million already in bookings after a year. The system pulls footage from body-cam footage, and delivers translation tools, summaries of events, and completes paperwork at an incredible speed, making the job of teams faster, better, and ultimately safer for all involved.

Q1 2025 Strength

Looking at the numbers, it's been a great quarter for the company. Q1 2024 reported revenues of $604 million, with Software now making up over half this amount. This represents an exciting moment in the company, which is no longer just developing hardware, but is moving steadily into very high-margin sectors with ease.

With gross margins now at 64%, management will be looking to expand offerings to companies only utilising some of the tools on offer. With a foot in the door, more premium offerings can be delivered, building enduring relationships with companies and agencies across the spectrum of use case.

This trend looks to be well underway, with 123% in net revenue retention. If management can continue to focus on discipline in spending, which saw FCF generation move positive to $2.7 million after a negative Q1 in 2024, I see a great deal of success in subsequent reports.

The balance sheet also looks to be in a good place, with nearly $10 billion in bookings agreed, and plenty of demand to capture in the new and growing markets I noted previously. Guidance for the next year reflects this optimism, with CAPEX climbing alongside the firm's ambitions in AI and next-gen hardware.

All in all, the Q1 2025 report ticks all the boxes. Management clearly knows the direction it wants to go in, is absolutely firing on all cylinders operationally, and looks to be building presence in areas which command huge potential, both in scalability and margins.

Looking at it alongside the share price though, and questions still remain for me on whether management can continue to meet the near-flawless expectations being put on the company by the market.

Valuation Risk

To establish how these expectations align with reality, I carried out a discounted cash flow calculation, which the following inputs:

  • FY25 revenues of $2.7 billion (based on guidance)
  • 22% FCF margins (based on recent performance)
  • 20% 5Y revenue growth
  • WACC of 8%
  • 3% terminal growth beyond 5Y.

By considering these baseline variables, I get an implied value of the shares at $570, about 25% down from the current level.

In a slightly more optimistic scenario, where FCF grows by about 25%, and the terminal growth rate settles at 4%, this value rises to about $675. This is notably still below the current level, and takes leaves little room for operational challenges or bumps in the road economically. In such a scenario, where margins are flat, and growth slows to about 15%, the fair value would be closer to $470, bringing a genuine risk of major downside.

Looking across the market, albeit in slightly different sectors, but in SaaS names, nonetheless, many firms trade closer to the 10x mark for EV/Revenue, and offer a much more diversified operation, and wider revenue potential. Axon's EV/EBITDA also might raise a few eyebrows at 85x, even when looking at it as a software firm.

Public safety is generally a fairly stable sector, but as many investors will have learned the hard way, the technology sector is not. As the company becomes increasingly focussed on regulatory matters such as algorithmic policing, or data privacy, these high-margin opportunities could quickly become a major headache in certain regions. If the market is already pricing in widespread adoption in areas where such technology is considered imposing and disruptive, it could be a genuine challenge for the valuation to stay in place.

From the analyst coverage, it feels like Wall Street is well aware that the valuation is higher than it maybe should be for now. Sentiment is generally positive, but an average price target of just about $701 indicates that there is a healthy amount of scepticism in place after such an extended rally.

Firing On All Cylinders, But Overvalued

Axon is clearly doing all the right things to build capabilities in a critical field, both for public safety and for global security. Over a long history, it has saved lives, made policing more transparent, and helped law enforcement to make a highly complex environment slightly more easy to manage. However, I think the market has now gone a little too far, pricing in revenues well in advance of their adoption in challenging markets and regions, which could well lead to major disappointment.

That said, the business is looking strong, is seeing strong demand ahead, and has management with the resources needed to continue this success.

My worry remains with the valuation, and with investor expectations near an all-time high for the company, any problems encountered could spell bad news for the share price. As a result, I'll be keeping my Hold rating on the firm, keeping a close eye on how the market reacts to the coming quarters.

data.title: Unilever's Short-Sightedness May Hurt Long-Term Business
data.description:
Unilever (UL)(OTCPK:UNLYF) blindsided. My previous article sang high praises for the corporation’s sustainability efforts. Now, not only has Unilever gone back on its green ambitions — which effectively motivated its corporate model for well over a decade — it has also been embroiled in a series of ethical lapses, with ensuing activist criticism, consumer boycotts and legal battles.

Many of Unilever’s top markets are already focal points for consumer advocacy. And waiting on the sidelines as consumer consciousness inevitably matures in otherwise unimpacted markets is a risky proposition that might end up hurting the stock.

Strategy copout

Starting from the top with strategy, Unilever has pushed back its original deadlines on key environmental and social metrics including sustainable packaging and financial backing for suppliers. Some pledges were dropped altogether.

The earlier commitments in 2025 to cut virgin plastic by 50% and to make all packaging recyclable/reusable/compostable have been deferred to 2028 (by 40% at most) and 2035 respectively. Supporting a living wage for direct contractors is now going to cover only 50% of qualifying suppliers.

Management argues that a focused approach on fewer ESG goals will yield better results. The critics, however, are convinced CEO Hein Schumacher is simply pandering to market pressure to forgo green targets in favor of more business-minded goals.

Except it does not work that way. Unilever, as one of the top plastic polluters in the world, can hardly afford to waffle over sustainability. In the UK, where it is listed, it is already being investigated for greenwashing. Business and reputational damage from eroding consumer trust and brand value are genuine threats.

Regulations on sustainability are becoming stricter in most jurisdictions, with the EU in the lead. If Unilever ends up lagging in compliance, it would risk fines, forced product redesigns, market restrictions and ultimately competitive disadvantages — especially given the group’s size and operational complexity.

Deterioration all around

Under Paul Polman, who first introduced the idea of “purpose beyond profit”, Unilever had a rather uneventful decade. His successor Alan Jope had it harder and was blamed for everything from falling growth rates to the company’s policies on controversial matters.

First off, still on plastics, Unilever was exposed for lobbying against laws to ban flexible packaging items in multiple low-income countries where communities and ecosystems are most affected. This despite persistent public messaging against plastic sachets.

Beyond environmental issues, Unilever’s handling of politically charged social issues has been abysmal. And nothing is more telling of that than its feud with Ben & Jerry’s, its longtime ice cream subsidiary that had been promised the freedom to safeguard its social mission.

Unilever’s meddling in Ben & Jerry’s business started in 2021 when the brand decided to stop selling ice cream in the occupied Palestinian territories. Unilever overturned the decision and in subsequent years grew increasingly authoritarian, controlling the ice cream maker’s social media posts and even donations.

The confrontation between the two reached a peak in March this year after Unilever unilaterally fired Ben & Jerry’s CEO David Stever. The parent cited performance issues but could not elaborate given the subsidiary’s top-of-the-line growth record and brand strength since acquisition.

Financials

In contrast, Unilever as a group is in decline. Net income has fallen for two years in a row, down by about 11% in both 2023 and 2024. Revenue has plateaued since 2022. Performance in big emerging markets such as China, Brazil and Indonesia has been particularly challenging.

In Muslim-majority Indonesia, for one, Unilever has been a target of widespread consumer boycotts over its perceived public support for Israel. This led to double-digit declines in sales from late 2023 right up to the latest results in 2024. In fact, the whole of Southeast Asia is reporting a similar negative trend with Unilever losing share to local brands.

The latest Q1 2025 trading statement corroborated the group's poor financial standing. Overall sales declined (-0.9%), with weakness in emerging markets (volume down 0.1%). Home Care and Foods continue to be sluggish (with underlying sales growth of 0.9% and 1.6% respectively), compared to better performing Beauty, Personal Care and Ice Cream (4-5%).

Moving forward, given low price and volume growth, margin improvement will remain in focus. Efficiency measures have been helping Unilever maintain steady cash flows. But net income is still under pressure, also due to restructuring risks.

Amplifying the existing campaigns is the renewed call to boycott Unilever over its treatment of Ben & Jerry’s. The group surely hopes that its spinning off the ice cream division will reduce the associated headaches. Just that the harm done to Unilever's name has only added to its already poor reputation.

Management itself admits that there is "a long way to go" to restore confidence and leadership, as brand performance lags far behind historical highs. The question one must ask is whether shedding social responsibility is the optimal way for Unilever to find its way back to the top.

Brand equity

Unilever’s weakening brand value is attested by a range of sources. From 2022 to 2023, it fell out of the top 20 in the "Brand Finance Top 50 Most Valuable Food Brands" and dropped from 22nd position to 86th in the "Global Top 100 Brand Corporations" by the European Brand Institute.

Historical metrics as of the latest available publications show a similar downward trend. The group’s brand value decreased from $4bn in 2019 to $3bn in 2023. In Fortune’s "World’s Most Admired Companies", Unilever was downgraded from 16th place in 2018 to 46th in 2023.

Meanwhile, the brand value of Ben & Jerry’s alone is approximated at $1bn. It has been in the top 5 of the Brand 500 Authenticity Index for years (number 1 in Food & Beverage in 2024).

Stock

The stock has been underperforming, trailing behind the US market. With dividends included, the returns look slightly better: 45% over the past five years. Many investors must have been reassured by the outgoing CEO who proved to be a pure business type and did not shy away from controversy. The new CEO is expected to emulate him, plus more.

Conclusion

Unilever is “transforming”, no doubt. But not necessarily for the better. What the refreshed leadership seems to be overlooking is the fact that new generations of consumers — from the US and Europe to Indonesia and India — are much more attuned to social and environmental justice than their predecessors ever were. Improving performance in any serious way is not all about getting rid of inconvenient brands and cost cuts at an accelerated pace. Progress will be impossible to sustain without active efforts to avoid getting caught on the wrong side of history, be it the environment or human rights.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sell Tempus AI Stock Before The Cooldown Hits
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Health tech play Tempus AI (NASDAQ:TEM) has been on fire lately, posting a massive 42% gain over the past month alone. Sure, easing macro headwinds helped, but the bulk of those gains come from its superb operational performance and smart M&A moves. However, that kind of growth doesn't come cheap, and after the stock's recent run-up, it's now sitting at a sky-high valuation. At over 14 times trailing sales and 33 times book value, there's barely any room for disappointment. Hence, it's probably best to wait for a likely cooldown before loading up more.

Tempus AI at a Glance

Tempus AI looks to blend Big Data and AI to effectively revolutionize precision medicine. It's built one of the world's biggest collections of clinical and molecular data, along with the tools to use it for personalized care. Moreover, it's one of the few companies genuinely putting AI to use in the healthcare space to significantly improve patient outcomes. Initially, it started off in oncology (cancer-related) and has since then expanded its tentacles across multiple medical domains, serving 3,000 U.S. provider sites.

Moreover, the platform effectively runs on three primary 'wheels'. First up is its Genomics division, which generates roughly 75% of its quarterly sales. Think of Genomics as running DNA checkups for clinicians across multiple divisions in oncology, cardiology, radiology, and more. Secondly, there's the Data and Services segment, which pulls in roughly 25% of quarterly sales, while processing test info, stripping out IDs, licensing to drugmakers, and helping recruit trial volunteers. Finally, there's the fast-growing AI applications segment, which essentially works as an 'app store' for doctors. Though relatively nascent, Tempus's AI toolkit is the fastest-growing segment for the business.

What separates Tempus from the pack is that it offers a one-stop solution for its clients. Not only does it test DNA or run tests, but it also collects a ton of data and then uses AI to spot patterns and insights. Hence, it's part diagnostic lab, part data-crunching firm, and part AI software shop that's built to help doctors efficiently tailor treatments. Sure, its rivals like Guardant with its liquid biopsy expertise or Foundation with Roche's global reach pose challenges, but Tempus AI's all-rounder platform is second to none.

Earnings Review

Tempus has been dishing out some head-turning numbers on both lines of late.

In Q1, its revenue jumped 75.4% YOY to $255.7 million, nearly double its showing last year. Results across each of its segments were impressive, with Genomics leading the pack, posting 89% YOY sales growth to $193.8 million. Also, Data and Services saw tremendous expansion in the 40%+ range as well, complementing the Genomics division's success.

Apart from revenues, Tempus's margins continue moving in the right direction. In Q1, gross profits came in at $155.2 million, roughly double its gross profit a year ago, highlighting the benefits of scale. Moreover, it also reported a $68 million net loss, which doesn't sound as steep considering the ramp-up in activity (for context, it posted $64.7 million in loss in Q1 2024). Also, its adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed substantially from $43.9 million in Q1 2024 to just $16.2 million this year.

By the close of last year, it parked $341 million in cash, while continuing to fund its expansion internally, peppering in some debt. In powering its Ambry Genetics deal, it had to tap Ares Capital for $300 million in debt, but overall, its positioning remains strong. In addition to that, its management is targeting a positive $5 million adjusted EBITDA for 2025, a massive swing from the $110 million loss from last year.

Speaking of Ambry, Tempus closed out Q1 with two big acquisitions, with Ambry being the headline deal. Ambry brings its incredible hereditary-disease testing franchise to Tempus, which effectively complements its oncology focus. Pre-acquisition, Ambry posted $100 million+ in revenues, which points to a healthy expansion in Tempus's test menu and client base. As a consequence, its management raised its 2025 revenue guidance to roughly $1.25 billion, marking an 80% increase.

In broadening its moat further, Tempus scooped up Deep 6 AI, a tool that quickly scouts patients for clinical trials. It searches health records covering 30 million patients to match them with the right people to the right trials. Hence, it's another feather in the cap for Tempus, adding healthy incremental reach to the platform.

What Lies Ahead for Tempus AI?

As we look ahead, there's plenty to look forward to with Tempus AI. It's playing across multiple spheres, and with global oncology testing alone at a $15 billion TAM, its current sales barely scratch the surface.

Moreover, with the Ambry acquisition, Tempus has now expanded into germline testing, having covered somatic (tumor) over the years. Awareness around "know your genes" is rising across the industry, pushing testing volumes up by double-digit margins. Hence, with its updated repertoire, Tempus can effectively triple its addressable physician base, covering patients at prevention, early-detection, and treatment stages.

Also, Tempus has already shown the long-term potential of "AI-as-a-service," having won a reimbursement for its ECG-AF algorithm last year. Beyond cardiology, Tempus also has live AI pathology models for various biomarker detection and is introducing new ones in neurology and psychiatry.

On top of that, Big Pharma players like AstraZeneca and GSK are looking to make use of Tempus's data to fuel faster drug discovery and trial recruitment. Unsurprisingly, analysts estimate a $2 billion annual market for AI-driven data services by next year.

Stock Market Gains and Valuation Discussion

Tempus AI's journey has been nothing short of spectacular. Having gone public for $37 per share in June last year, its IPO raised an extraordinary $410.7 million. It closed out its first day of trading at $40.25, valuing its business at close to $6.6 billion with a TTM revenue base of $500 million. However, like with most IPOs, the stock dipped to around the mid-$20s by the back-end of the year.

2025 flipped the script, though, with the stock being backed by maverick investors like Cathie Wood. Tempus is now a top-ten holding in ARK's Innovation ETF (ARKK) and among the top-three names in the Genomic Revolution fund. Hence, since early 2025, the stock has skyrocketed to the $58 mark, and is arguably one of the hottest AI healthcare plays.

Moreover, as we look ahead, Tempus could potentially hit a roughly 55-cent EPS by 2027 and $1.12 the following year. This effectively puts its forward PE at around 115 times in 2027 and 56 times when considering its 2028 earnings performance. Hence, a lot of its current valuation hinges on its execution, if growth slows down, then we could see a striking pullback in its price.

Nevertheless, its current pricing metrics offer little room to maneuver. For instance, it's trading at 14.5 times its TTM EV multiple, compared to a 3.3x sector median. On a price-to-sales basis, it's trading at roughly 11.6 times TTM revenue, compared to the sector at 3.3x. Naturally, a big part of why its metrics are where they are is due to its monstrous recent rally.

Risks to the Thesis

On the flip side, Tempus AI's robust fundamentals make its apparent risks look a lot more like hitches. Sure, it's still burning through cash at this point, but analyst estimates suggest it's probably going to break even within the next couple of years. Moreover, its investment is fueling a powerful 75% top-line surge. On top of that, with over $341 million in its cash till, it's effectively locking in its expansion without impacting its balance sheet. Also, the Ambry Genetics and Deep 6 AI acquisitions are likely to be turbochargers for its top and bottom lines, offering cross-sell wins and greater provider reach. It's worth noting that its management has had a strong track record in seamlessly stitching acquisitions into its powerful offerings. Additionally, with the largest multimodal data libraries, Tempus AI's competitive advantage remains as compelling as ever.

Takeaway on Tempus AI Stock

Overall, it's important to understand that you're paying for that rocket ride with Tempus AI. Tempus AI stock is trading at a nosebleed valuation, and despite the compelling long-term upside, it's not worth investing at current levels. The recent run-up seems overdone, which makes it best to wait for a meaningful pullback before jumping in.

data.title: High Conviction Stocks For Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
April’s Aftermath: U.S. Downgrade

April 2025 was a rollercoaster for equities, triggered by sharp shifts in U.S. trade policy and escalating tensions with China. The April 2nd tariff announcement, known as ‘Liberation Day,’ sent shockwaves through markets, as tariffs were set at their highest levels in over 100 years. Wall Street’s fear gauge, the VIX, soared past 50, a level not seen since the pandemic.

Source Link

Another pendulum swing followed, as the tariff pause caused the S&P 500 to surge 9.52% on April 9th, its best day since 2001. These gains were quickly erased by a series of tariff tit-for-tats between the U.S. and China, raising fears of a full-blown trade war. Though the S&P 500 ultimately finished April only slightly in the red, and the recent U.S.-China negotiations sparked a five-day winning streak the week of May 11th, the underlying economic and fiscal picture remains fragile.

The US economy contracted by 0.3% in Q1 2025, marking its first negative growth since 2022. This weaker showing was exacerbated by a surge in imports ahead of the tariff deadline.

Source Link

The housing market, a traditional economic bellwether, has also shown signs of softening.

Existing-home Sales Have Slowed

Source Link

Last week, Moody’s downgraded the U.S. credit rating from Aaa to Aa1, citing the widening deficit and rising debt, which are impacting stocks, bonds, and the U.S. dollar and leaving the fiscal outlook clouded. Interest rates have held steady through the first three FOMC meetings of the year, with the expectation that they will stay the same in July as policymakers wait for more clarity on the fallout from tariffs.

94% Probability of No Change For The Next Fed Meeting in June

Source Link

Current economic indicators put markets in a vulnerable state of uncertainty. Should the 90-day tariff suspension end without a resolution, or if negotiation details disappoint investors, market volatility will likely resurface. This could be further exacerbated if the U.S. officially enters a recession.

Recession Risk Still Looms

Earlier this spring, projections for a U.S. recession were as high as 90%. The recent U.S.-China tariff truce has caused major brokerages to cut their recession forecasts down to 35% - 45%. The technical definition of a recession is two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth, and the first quarter’s contraction keeps the current risk for a recession elevated. With the distinct possibility of renewed trade tensions and a possible recession, investors need to be forearmed with a strategy for staying invested and capitalizing on opportunities during the downturn to avoid missing out on rebounds. Historically, the market’s best days typically occur within two weeks of the 10 worst days.

Source Link

In April, SA Quant identified three resilient stocks positioned to weather an economic downturn in Weaponizing Tariffs: Best Stocks To Buy for A Recession. Building on this analysis, we've expanded our recession and tariff-minded portfolio with five additional selections. These companies offer defensive characteristics and growth potential even in volatile markets and recession concerns. Our recommendations provide investors with more options to fortify their portfolios against potential economic headwinds across multiple sectors.

What Makes a High-Conviction, Tariff-Resistant Stock?

In this environment of trade uncertainty and economic precarity, it is important to identify stocks that can weather trade disruptions and economic turbulence. We’ve identified companies that, in addition to their excellent Quant ratings and factor grades, meet one or more of the below criteria:

  • Domestic Production and Revenues: U.S. domiciled companies that manufacture and generate most of their revenue from within the U.S. are less vulnerable to input price increases and softening demand as a result of tariff hocks.

  • Digital-First Services: Firms whose core offerings are digital or service-based are less exposed to supply chain disruptions and rising input costs.

  • Diversified Production: Businesses with flexible or localized supply chains and minimal reliance on imported goods can better sidestep tariff-related shocks.

  • Defensive Sectors: Sectors such as consumer staples have historically shown more resilience during economic downturns due to their essential nature.

Let’s look at five top Quant-rated stocks that are well-suited for tariff uncertainty and a possible recession.

  1. Philip Morris International Inc. (PM)

  • Market Capitalization: $271.83B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Tobacco

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 1 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 1 out of 8

PM stands out as a tariff-resistant stock due to the company's resilient business model and inelastic demand, which allow for price increases without significantly affecting sales. PM’s exceptional pricing power generates stable cash flow, enabling it to be a high-quality dividend-paying stock that offers a 3.09% FWD yield and stellar dividend grades.

PM Dividend Grades

The company's smoke-free product ZYN has been particularly successful, with U.S. shipments increasing 53% year-over-year as production capacity expanded. PMI manufactures products like ZYN nicotine pouches domestically in the U.S. for the U.S. market and sources tobacco globally, reducing vulnerability to import tariffs and supply chain disruptions. This resilient growth has driven outstanding profitability, reflected in a 42% EBITDA margin, more than 200% above the sector median, and $11.63 billion in cash from operations.

PM Profitability Grade

PM delivered standout Q1 results with double-digit organic net revenue growth and a 13% increase in adjusted EPS. While its valuation appears stretched, the forward PEG ratio of 2.1x represents a 23% discount relative to the sector. Analysts have unanimously endorsed the stock with 13 upward FY1 revisions in the past 90 days and zero downward adjustments. This combination of resilient demand, pricing power, and regional manufacturing strategy positions Philip Morris as a financially strong, tariff-resistant stock with continued capital appreciation potential for shareholders.

2. CareTrust REIT, Inc. (CTRE)

  • Market Capitalization: $5.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Health Care REITs

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 4 out of 174

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 1 out of 17

CareTrust REIT is # 1 Quant-ranked Health Care REIT specializing in skilled nursing facilities and senior housing across the U.S. The company has outperformed the S&P 500 YTD, demonstrating resilience underpinned by robust fundamentals and long-term demand drivers in the healthcare sector, which tends to be less sensitive to economic cycles.

In Q1 2025, the company completed investments totaling $47 million at approximately 10% yield. The company raised its full-year 2025 guidance, with normalized FFO per share projected between $1.69 and $1.73, and normalized FAD per share between $1.73 and $1.77. CTRE’s growth trajectory is reflected in the company’s ‘A+’ Growth grade, which includes a FWD AFFO Growth of more than 280% above the REITs sector.

CTRE’s profitability is also top-tier, with a TTM AFFO margin of 103% coupled with an interest coverage ratio of 7.6x. These standout profitability metrics help ensure the company’s attractive 4.65% FWD yield has a measure of safety. The REIT’s overall ‘A-’ Dividend Safety Grade is complemented by its ‘A+’ Growth Grade, which includes a 5Y Dividend Growth Rate CAGR of 5.43%.

CTRE Dividend Growth Grade

CTRE is a solid income-generating asset with defensive characteristics built to weather economic disruptions that might be brought on thanks to its superior growth and profitability, as well as dividend characteristics.

3. Stride Inc. (LRN)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.69B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Education Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 477

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 3 out of 25

Stride, Inc. is a U.S. edtech company that has returned more than 50% YTD, benefitting from its digital-first, service-based business model in the uncertain tariff environment. Furthermore, the company’s revenue structure makes it relatively immune to shifts in federal policy.

“I know there's lots of discussion about federal funding and the impact that could have on Stride. I want to reiterate what we've said last quarter, well less than 5% of our overall revenues come from federal resources,” said Donna Blackman CFO of Stride.

The company’s Q3 FY25 earnings underscore LRN’s exceptional growth, driven by a deluge of student enrollment, particularly in its career learning segment. Revenue climbed double-digits Y/Y, and adjusted EBITDA grew more than 40%.

LRN Growth Grade

Effective cost management and operational gains have helped drive the company’s solid ‘B-’ Profitability grade. Highlights include a net income margin and ROE that are 199% and 106% above the sector median, respectively. As one might expect, LRN’s incredible run-up in the last year has diminished the company’s overall valuation. However, the company’s 0.5x TTM PEG (nearly a third below the sector median) suggests the Stride could have further room to run. LRN’s consistent operational execution and relative protection from policy execution make this a solid, high-conviction consumer discretionary stock.

4. Pilgrim's Pride Corporation (PPC)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.07B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Packaged Foods and Meats

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 13 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 2 out of 51

Pilgrim’s is one of the world’s largest poultry producers and currently ranks as the #2 Quant-ranked Packaged Foods and Meats stock. PPC’s strong defense against trade disruptions stems from its geographic diversification and vertically integrated supply chain. With operations across Mexico and Europe, the company controls multiple production stages from farm to finished product, allowing it to distribute to regional markets directly. Strong demand for chicken has also helped bolster the company against potential tariff headwinds.

“To date, other trading partners around the world continue to navigate tariffs, enabling strong demand. This is partially attributed to the attractive value of chicken compared to more expensive proteins, along with disease and supply issues in other chicken-producing countries,” said Fabio Sandri, President & Chief Executive Officer of PPC.

PPC demonstrated robust growth in Q1 2025, with adjusted EBITDA surging 62% Y/Y. The company’s forward-looking earnings look attractive as well, with FWD EPS Diluted Growth at 49%, which is more than 780% above the sector median. The company also offers excellent profitability, with a 37% ROE vs. the sector’s 11% and $8.71 in cash per share.

PPC has soundly outperformed the S&P 500 on a trailing one-year basis, delivering an overall ‘B-’ Momentum grade. Remarkably, the company has also been able to maintain an attractive valuation despite the gains; PPC trades at a 94% discount in terms of its TTM PEG ratio.

PPC vs. the S&P 500 1-Year Trading Chart

With strong profitability, discounted valuation metrics, and diversified operations, PPC is not only a consumer staples standout but also relatively resistant to tariff impacts.

5. Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER)

  • Market Capitalization: $185.42B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Passenger Ground Transportation

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 5 out of 617

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 5/22/25): 1 out of 8

UBER has expanded significantly beyond its initial ride-hailing days to include food and grocery delivery, and freight logistics. The company’s core operations are local and digital, relying on independent couriers in each market. In Q1, monthly active consumers grew 14%, while trips climbed in tandem by 18%. As a result, the company reported a record adjusted EBITDA that was 35% higher Y/Y, contributing to the company’s stellar profitability grade. Uber sports a TTM ROE that is 500% above the sector median, while delivering more than $8B in cash from operations.

UBER Profitability Grade

Beyond Uber’s core services, the company invests heavily in autonomous vehicles, launching new partnerships, and is seeing high utilization in early deployments. The company’s progress has added to optimism around the stock; UBER has returned an incredible 46% YTD, while securing 39 FY1 up revisions in the last 90 days versus one downward.

UBER Revisions Grade

Looking at the company from a valuation perspective, UBER trades in line with the broader industrials sector, but offers a very attractive forward PEG ratio of 1.1x vs. the sector’s 1.7x. Uber is a strong tariff-resistant play with excellent growth prospects driven by disciplined execution and a clear strategy.

Conclusion

April 2025 brought extreme market volatility following major U.S. tariff announcements that sent the VIX soaring past 50, with the S&P 500 finishing slightly negative for the month. Despite a reprieve in early May sparked by a U.S.-China tariff truce, many underlying vulnerabilities persist. The U.S. economy shrank in Q1, while Moody’s recently downgraded the U.S. economy’s credit rating. Though recession projections have decreased following trade negotiations, risks remain elevated with potential further deterioration if the 90-day tariff suspension ends without resolution.

SA Quant has identified five stocks well-suited for an environment of tariff uncertainty and potential economic downturn. These tariff-resistant picks share key characteristics: domestic revenue streams, digital-first service models, diversified production capabilities with minimal import dependence, and presence in historically defensive sectors like consumer staples—all designed to weather economic uncertainty and trade tensions.

Our five stock picks possess solid fundamentals and valuations, and positive growth outlooks, and their Factor Grades and Quant Ratings can help you make prudent investment decisions in an uncertain environment. Finding knowledgeable investment resources is a great way to be a successful investor in volatile markets. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help you find stocks that achieve diversification into desired sectors you like, including Top Consumer Staple Stocks.

data.title: United States Steel: Extreme Overvaluation Calls For Extreme Caution
data.description:
Today, I want to take a brief look at United States Steel Corporation (NYSE:X) and understand why I rate it as a 'Strong Sell'. Many of you probably know that there have been talks regarding the purchase of the company by a Japanese steel firm, Nippon Steel, at $55.00 per share. The thing is, that despite these being just talks and not any signed sort of agreements, it is the main reason to why X is up by a staggering 420.00% through the past 5 years.

Of course, if X were indeed to get acquired at $55.00 a share, there would be plenty of upsides, but the fact that currently, all that this buyout is based on are talks, in this article we will focus on the option that the buyout will not go through. My thesis is that due to being extremely overvalued and having serious flaws in its fundamentals, despite still climbing in stock price, the company is a bubble ready to explode.

Price Growth is Completely Unjustified by Financial Growth

Over the past 5 years, X's revenue is up by 60.56%, from $9.74B in 2020 to $15.64B in 2024. The thing is that most of this growth was accounted for in a single year (2020), and since 2021, the revenue is actually down by 22.86%.

The net income has also been falling at a high rate, down from $4.17B in 2021 to $384.00M in 2024, a decrease of 90.80%. FCF is also down from $3.23B in 2021 to -$1.37B in 2024.

Some might think that this is just short-term growth and that, in the long term, the company's financials are growing, but that isn't the case. Over the past 10 years, the revenue is up by only 31.39%, accounting for a CAGR of 2.76%, which wouldn't even cover the long-term average inflation rate of 3.28%. Net income is up from negative $1.64B to $384.00M in 2024, but as you can see below, this growth is extremely unpredictable.

Essentially, what I am trying to say is that in the short term or through the long term, it doesn't matter; the company isn't growing. Everything just comes down to them having a good or a bad year and making an unpredictable amount of sales, whether it is in the negative or positive.

Unhealthy Amount of Debt

Something I want to point out further is that X has a lot of debt compared to their average FCF-generating abilities. Over the past 5 years, their average yearly FCF has been sitting at $506.00M, meaning that to pay off their $5.30B in LTL, it would take them around 25 years, that is, while paying out their dividend. This much debt, of course, makes the company quite vulnerable to financial downturns.

Talking about dividends, usually, when a company has low growth to offer, they at least have a high dividend yield, but that isn't the case with X. Currently, their yield is 0.49%, and historically, this dividend has only been decreasing, falling from $0.25 in 1991 to $0.05 in 2024.

To sum everything up so far, before getting to the valuation, the company has low to no growth, high debt, an insignificant dividend yield, and, as we will see in a moment, is unjustifiably overvalued.

What we can do is compare an investment into X to an investment into US treasury bonds. If the acquisition did go through, the upside is still quite limited, offering only slightly south of a 25.00% gain. Considering the risk of the acquisition not going through, a 100.00% safe investment into bonds currently yielding 4.75% that guarantee the same return within 5 years seems like a more sensible option.

Valuation

Looking at the PE ratio, it is currently sitting at 142.00, extremely high for a company that shows almost no growth at all. Looking at the trailing PE through the past 5 years, you will see that it has been increasing with the stock price, meaning that the company has been getting more and more expensive despite the only upside being talks about an acquisition. This I find totally unjustifiable, as it shows that X's price is indeed based more on news than on the fundamentals and financial generating abilities.

In fact, when we compare X to three of its competitors, CRS, CMC, and GGB, we will find that their average PE is just 40.77, more than 3 times lower than X's PE, leading to an intrinsic value of only $11.82.

Looking at the second valuation model, the Graham's Formula, I will take the 5-year average EPS of $2.47 (much higher than the current EPS of $0.29) and predict a future CAGR of 3.33% based on the historical growth. This will give us an intrinsic value of $26.88, 34.73% lower than the current trading price of $41.18, taking into account that the 5Y Average EPS is the highest the company has ever seen and will probably not reach such heights in a very long time.

Nevertheless, for the third and final valuation, we will look to the DCF model, where I will again take the highest average FCF ($506.00M) the company has ever seen in consecutive 5 years, and based off of that, calculate the FFCF using an unrealistically high FCF CAGR of 7.30% into the next 10Y +. This will give us an intrinsic value of $16.20, accounting for the WACC of 12.31%.

Overall, when we average out the three values, we will get an intrinsic value of $18.30, 55.56% lower than the current trading price of $41.18, and that is when using, in my opinion, unrealistically high growth CAGRs.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I believe that I have very well highlighted the reasons to why I rate United States Steel Corporation as a 'Strong Sell'. Their high debt, low financial growth, overvaluation, and below-average dividend and dividend growth at all don't justify their 5Y price rally.

Investing into the US treasury bonds, taking into account risk factors, would yield much higher returns at 4.75%, than an investment into X. In my eyes it is more likely that an acquisition at $55.00 a share will not go through, which means that when the hype around these talks calms, I would expect the company to fall down in stock price, and by a lot, justifying my 'Strong Sell' rating.

data.title: Bitcoin ETFs: How To Have Your BTC Cake And Eat It Too
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is becoming a mainstream asset with a steady rise in institutional ownership, but its high volatility can create stress for your portfolio. In a recent pullback, the cryptocurrency plunged about 30% from its January high before soaring again.

Is there a way to gain full BTC exposure while completely hedging its violent downside? Probably not, but it’s possible to enjoy a decent amount of upside and still be protected against extreme losses. A covered call strategy involving a bitcoin ETF is how we may express cautious optimism on the cryptocurrency.

Large and liquid ETFs, such as iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC) and Franklin Bitcoin ETF (EZBC), can serve as the underlying asset which pairs with short calls. For example, we can set up a covered call combo through buying 100 shares of IBIT and selling one at-the-money call option on the ETF at the same time. Thanks to high implied volatility, the premium from selling long-term calls may be as rich as 40% of the underlying value.

IBIT, the largest bitcoin ETF, is managed by BlackRock and is designed to track the spot BTC price with a correlation close to 1. It has a modest 0.25% expense ratio, which is the same as FBTC's but slightly higher than EZBC's.

Institutional sway

A game changer for bitcoin investment is the rising participation by institutions, including asset managers, governments, public and private enterprises. Over the last five years, the cryptocurrency’s institutional ownership has surged from below 2% to more than 14%, as shown in the following chart.

The institutional embrace of bitcoin has boosted both its value and legitimacy as an asset class. Global asset managers like BlackRock and Fidelity have launched Bitcoin ETFs, while sovereign funds are increasingly adding cryptocurrency to their portfolios. Tesla and many other companies have accumulated bitcoins in their treasury reserves.

As the institutional weight continues to grow, the cryptocurrency’s volatility looks set to decline over the long term. A drop in volatility would work in favor of covered calls by eroding the time value of options.

Deflationary appeal

Bitcoin is increasingly seen as the "digital gold" and a hedge against fiat currency devaluation. A key advantage of bitcoin over fiat money is the cryptocurrency’s limited supply, capped at 21 million coins, which makes it inherently deflationary. Only about 5% of total coins are left to be mined.

Let’s use the aforementioned example of covered calls to illustrate expected returns in various BTC price scenarios at maturity. The strategy involves 100 shares of IBIT, bought at $60 each, and one short call expiring end-2027 with a $60 strike. Selling the call can generate a premium of $2,360. Creating these positions was possible based on market prices as of May 20, when BTC traded at around $105,000. The rich premium is driven by the ETF’s high implied volatility, as well as strong demand for leverage among bullish crypto investors.

When the call matures, this strategy will generate a profit of about 40%, including interest earned on the cash premium, if BTC stays at or above $105,000. The payoff will be smaller if the cryptocurrency trades below the initial level, and will turn into losses if it drops more than 40% from $105,000. There’s minimal counterparty risk because the cash premium is received upfront.

Those who are more risk-averse may consider adding an out-of-the-money put to completely hedge the downside. However, the put cost may significantly reduce the upside. For instance, adding a put with the same maturity and a $46 strike will limit the maximum profit at $1,360, or about 23% of the initial IBIT value.

Alternatively, risk-takers could max out the upside potential by using the call premium to buy additional IBIT shares, which in turn could cover more short calls. This could boost the initial IBIT position by nearly 65% and inflate the maximum profit by the same proportion. However, the downside would be similarly magnified due to the cash premium being used up. For instance, if BTC dropped to zero, there would be a 100% loss.

Key risks

A sharp decline in BTC prices over the call option’s life – more than 40% from the initial level – will result in losses for this strategy. A global recession may trigger selloffs in bitcoin as a risk asset, and the use of leverage which is common among crypto traders could make things worse. Regulatory crackdowns could also lead to panic selling, as they did in the past, although governments have generally adopted a less hostile stance toward BTC in the last few years.

Bitcoin has a history of extreme volatility and dramatic crashes, such as an 80%-plus dive in 2017-18 and another 70%-plus slump in 2021-22.

And, despite its popularity, bitcoin is not without competition. Ethereum, Solana and other cryptocurrencies may challenge BTC’s dominance in certain use cases.

Conclusion

A covered call strategy involving bitcoin ETFs allows investors to gain exposure to the cryptocurrency, while hedging a large part of its downside. Rising institutional ownership and a supply cap combine to boost bitcoin’s appeal, but volatile trading remains a drawback of the cryptocurrency. The strategy takes advantage of high premiums for BTC options, which help ensure reasonable upside potential, as well as downside protection. Key risks include potential BTC selloffs in a recession, regulatory crackdowns and competition from other crypto assets.

data.title: BGR: OPEC+ Opening The Spigot Could Lead To An Oil Glut
data.description:
It's been quite a while since I last wrote about the BlackRock Energy & Resources Trust (NYSE:BGR). At the time, my assessment of the fund was that it may deliver disappointing long-term total returns, as it gives up a portion of its upside in exchange for upfront option premiums. At the same time, BGR's covered-call strategy exposes investors to downside returns if energy equities were to decline.

Since my article, the BGR fund has delivered 13.0% total returns, far below that of the S&P 500 Index, which has returned 32.2% on price alone (Figure 1). So my cautious view on the BGR fund was partially justified.

However, with the benefit of hindsight, let us reassess the other salient points of my article and discuss the outlook for energy equities and the BGR fund in the coming quarters.

Brief Fund Overview

The BlackRock Energy & Resources Trust is a closed-end fund ("CEF") that aims to provide total returns through current income and capital appreciation by investing in stocks of energy and natural resource companies. The main differentiator of BGR's strategy is that it uses a 'call-writing overlay' to generate premium income to enhance distributions to unitholders.

The BGR fund currently has $340 million in net assets, a modest decline from $396 million in net assets when I last reviewed the fund. This suggests the fund is losing popularity, and investors may be seeking their energy exposure elsewhere. The BGR fund charges a 1.23% gross expense ratio.

Portfolio Update

Figure 2 shows the sector allocation of the BGR fund as of April 30, 2025. The BGR fund's largest allocation is in integrated energy companies, with 52.4% of the portfolio, compared to 51.0% previously (May 2023). BGR's allocation to exploration and production companies (E&Ps) has notably been reduced to 19.1% (compared to 25.7%), while the allocation to distribution companies (i.e. pipelines) has been boosted to 18.7% (compared to 11.2%). Oil services at 3.8% and refining and marketing at 2.2% round out BGR's sector exposures.

BGR has written call options on ~1/3 of its portfolio, or 31.5%, which is fairly consistent with the 32.4% previously (Figure 3).

BGR Has Relatively Outperformed

In my prior article, I explained why I did not like the BGR fund's covered-call strategy as it applies to volatile energy equities. In my opinion, energy stocks tend to be autocorrelated, i.e., they tend to go on multi-month rallies and declines. By systematically writing call options, investors may be truncating upside returns while exposing themselves to downside capture.

However, a covered-call strategy can outperform a buy-and-hold strategy if stocks broadly trade sideways, as the covered-call can earn option premiums without too much underperformance from large rallies or losses from large declines. This is actually what happened since my last article in August 2023, as energy equities, represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) have been flat, while the BGR fund has returned 12.1% (Figure 4).

But Distribution Was Not 'Covered'

In my last article, I noted that the BGR fund's distribution yield, at 6.3%, looked high relative to the fund's earnings power. Fast-forward to 2025, the BGR fund has actually raised its monthly distribution to $0.10 or a 9.4% forward yield (Figure 5).

However, I fear this distribution rate is far too high for the BGR fund. Historically, the BGR fund has only earned 6.0% annualized over 3 years, so the fund appears to be overpaying its distribution by liquidating net asset value ("NAV"), a long-term unsustainable practice (Figure 6).

In fact, the BGR fund's long-term distribution policies appear to be questionable, as the fund's NAV has amortized from over $32 in 2011 to just $13.34 today (Figure 7).

As I have written extensively in past articles, funds that do not earn their distributions are characterized by amortizing NAVs, and are called 'return of principal' funds, and should be avoided.

OPEC Appears to Be Going for Market Share

Stepping back and considering energy prices overall, I have turned pessimistic on energy prices in recent months, as oil prices have broken down from a large symmetrical triangle pattern, and appear on track to test the lower range of a descending channel near ~$50 / barrel (Figure 8).

The biggest driver behind oil weakness appears to be OPEC+, which recently surprised markets by increasing oil production quotas by 411k boed in June, above market expectations. Investors were expecting more modest actions by OPEC+ given global economic uncertainty due to President Trump's tariffs.

Instead, led by Saudi Arabia, OPEC+ appears to be taking advantage of weak oil prices to apply further pressure on non-OPEC producers like North American shale, which had been growing production rapidly in the past few years at the Saudis' expense.

Risks to BGR

On the downside, should OPEC+ continue to ramp up production irrespective of demand signals, this could cause significant downside on energy prices and energy equities by extension. Furthermore, OPEC's target appears to be Western energy companies, which are predominantly the integrated and E&Ps owned by the BGR fund.

This situation could turn ugly, as North American shale producers generally need oil prices greater than $60 to fund their investments and dividends (Figure 9).

On the upside, there are rumblings of a possible strike by Israel on Iran's nuclear facilities. If the media reports are true and another regional war breaks out between Israel and Iran, then oil prices could squeeze higher, since Iran produces ~3% of the world's oil.

Conclusion

The BlackRock Energy & Resources Trust applies a call-writing overlay to energy equities to generate high current income. Overall, BGR's covered-call strategy has relatively outperformed in the past few quarters, as energy equities have tread water while BGR has earned option premium.

However, looking further out, I am concerned that Donald Trump's tariff policies may be hurting global economic growth, which will sap energy demand. At the same time, OPEC+ appears to be taking advantage of weak oil prices to apply pressure on North American energy producers by ramping up its production, potentially leading to a glut of oil and a decline in energy equities.

Given my negative outlook on the economy and oil prices, I recommend investors exercise caution with the BGR fund, and I am downgrading it to a 'Sell' rating.

data.title: Chipotle: This Beloved Brand Can't Justify Its Premium Anymore
data.description:
In the stock market, there have always been a few perennial favorite growth brands that investors have piled on into. The premise of these companies was that they could continue to spark outsized growth, no matter what the prevailing macroeconomic conditions were, helping to justify their large valuation premiums.

A couple of those seemingly ironclad brands have been torn down over the past year. Nike (NKE) is a big example, and Chipotle (CMG) is another. The fast-casual burrito chain has tipped into declining same-store sales growth as it faces down a tough consumer spending environment, bringing its share price down ~15% year to date.

Many investors may see Chipotle's recent declines as a rare opportunity to buy a stock that is perennially rising, but the question is: to what extent will Chipotle's fundamental cracks begin to erode the company's growth narrative and threaten its premium valuation?

I last wrote a bearish article on Chipotle in March, when the stock was still trading in the high $50s. Though the stock market has largely recouped the majority of its post-tariff declines, Chipotle is sitting decidedly lower on the back of a poor Q1 earnings print that showed a massive deceleration in same-store growth rates. I'm reiterating my sell rating here.

Here's a disclaimer: I'm a frequent Chipotle diner and a major fan of the brand. Especially in today's environment, the company strikes a great balance between value, healthy meals, and taste that few other chain dining brands can muster. But great brands don't always make great investments, and usually, it's valuation that causes the break.

Since a high price is the crux of my sell rating on Chipotle, let's start here. For one, we note that Chipotle is only expecting low single digit comp sales growth this year, which would already represent improvement from Q1 trends (in other words, perhaps already a stretch).

Wall Street is expecting Chipotle to hit $1.21 in pro forma EPS this year (+8% y/y) on 8% revenue growth, while next year the Street consensus is pegged at $1.44 in pro forma EPS (+19% y/y) on an acceleration to 13% revenue growth. This puts Chipotle's valuation multiples at:

  • 42x FY25 P/E
  • 35x FY26 P/E

Even if we believe that Chipotle isn't already saturated, that its next international country launch into Mexico yields incrementally, and that the company can surmount rising labor and food costs to deliver outsized margin gains, we have to ask ourselves: can we really justify paying ~40x earnings for this stock when most other major restaurant chains are trading much cheaper than Chipotle?

When it comes to investing in the restaurant space, I'd rather stick to a cheaper value option or take a bet on true high-flying growth stocks like CAVA (CAVA) or Sweetgreen (SG) that have more white space to infill and expand their store counts in the U.S. and multiply their earnings base, rather than Chipotle whose best days I believe are behind it.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q1 exposes a number of issues, from slowing sales growth to rising labor costs

Chipotle's first-quarter results, released in late April, provide little relief for the company's recent fundamental concerns. Take a look at the Q1 results below:

Chipotle's revenue grew 6% y/y to $2.88 billion, missing Wall Street's expectations of $2.94 billion (+9% y/y) by a wide three-point margin. Revenue growth rates cut in half relative to 13% y/y growth in Q4.

Of course, the only reason that Chipotle is able to grow at all is due to its pace of new restaurant openings (its guidance range of 315-345 new locations this year is a jump from 297 net-new stores added in FY24). On a comparable sales basis, however, which strips out the contribution of newly opened locations, we note that growth clocked in at -0.4%, a sharp 580bps deceleration from 5.4% comparable sales growth in Q4.

Underneath this poor result, the company noted that traffic actually declined by 230bps, partially offset by 190bps in pricing increases. This is an indication that consumers are finally noticing and pushing back against the chain's quiet, gradual price increases and that its ability to use pricing as a further lever to stimulate growth will be limited.

Unfortunately: Chipotle needs to raise prices to combat costs, and costs are rising faster than Chipotle's ability to push prices higher. We note that the company's restaurant-level operating margins (a measure of profitability without corporate overhead) declined 130bps y/y to 26.2%.

Chipotle's margin profile is still best-in-class (many restaurants are struggling with a low/mid-20s margin profile), but we note that the company is facing a particular bite from labor costs, which rose to 25.0% as a percentage of revenue (60bps higher y/y), driven in part by minimum wage increases in California (franchise chains are now subject to a $20 minimum wage versus the $16.50 statewide minimum, as of last April).

Gross margins are expected to remain under pressure in Q2 and beyond as tariff impacts hit. Per CFO Aiden Rymer's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

For Q2, we expect our cost of sales to be in the high 29% range as the mix benefit from Chipotle Honey Chicken will be more than offset by higher inflation across several items, the normalization of avocado prices and the impact of the newly enacted tariffs, including aluminum and the broad-based 10% tariff.

We estimate these tariffs will have an ongoing impact of about 50 basis points and due to inventory on hand, we anticipate a 20 basis point impact in Q2, which is included in our guidance. These estimates do not include any impact from the tariffs that were postponed or the 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada since our imports fall under the U.S. MCA exemption."

To me, Chipotle's main path to overcoming margin pressures is by increasing restaurant AUV/throughput. 80% of the company's new restaurant builds this year are planned to have a drive-through ("Chipotlane"), which could broaden Chipotle's customer base and improve throughput at non-peak meal times. However, with increasing macro pressures and consumers pulling back on discretionary spending, I find it more likely that comparable sales will decelerate further.

Key takeaways

It's a simple question that few investors will have the answer to: why pay a ~40x earnings multiple for a company that is barely growing? In my view, Chipotle's growth narrative has been broken, and the spotlight is now firmly on rising costs on all fronts: avocado inflation, rising labor rates, and even rising rents as a percentage of revenue. Chipotle's days of generating reliable double-digit earnings growth are behind it, and as such, I believe the stock's premium valuation multiple will also gradually wither. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.
